name: Kindergarten Mathematics — Semester 1
description: An 18-week, play-based Kindergarten pathway for counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10. Lessons are brief, hands-on, flexible, and designed for adult-guided learning.
learningGoals: Build confidence and curiosity with counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.
Communicate learning through talk, drawing, movement, play, or a simple creation.
Practice safe, kind, and responsible participation.

lessons.title: Notice and talk: counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10
lessons.objective: I can notice details and share a question about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10 through a close look at counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.

lessons.objective: I can notice details and share a question about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10 through a guided practice with counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.

lessons.objective: I can notice details and share a question about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10 through a small creation or demonstration about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.

units.title: Wonder and notice: counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10
units.essentialQuestion: What can we notice and wonder about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10?
outcomes: I can notice details and share a question about counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.
I can use words, pictures, movement, or materials to communicate my thinking.

activities.title: A close look at counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10
activities.learnerAction: With an adult or teacher nearby when needed, the learner participates in a close look at counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10, uses words, pictures, movement, or materials to communicate thinking, and cleans up safely.

successCriteria: The learner participates safely and respectfully in counting, comparing, and representing numbers to 10.
The learner communicates one observation, choice, or question using speech, drawing, movement, or a supported response.
